text: And those sample-sized bottles of champagne they were just giving away at that liquor store, the beach town before. I remember it so vividly—handing you one end of the blanket and shaking it out together—the thorny yellow grass we draped it over—a patch of refuge, we created—us plopping down, you already peeling the foil from a bottle—then succeeding, and handing it to me. We felt lucky, didn't we?
expected: I remember it so vividly—handing you one end of the blanket and shaking it out together—the thorny yellow grass we draped it over—a patch of refuge, we created—us plopping down, you already peeling the foil from a bottle—then succeeding, and handing it to me.
description: Multiple em dashes fragmenting single sentence without creating boundaries

text: We felt lucky, didn't we? Ahead of us, miles of cars sitting single-file, engines killed, stranded on a two-lane highway pinned between mountains and the Pacific ocean. What if the fire were to reach us?
expected: Ahead of us, miles of cars sitting single-file, engines killed, stranded on a two-lane highway pinned between mountains and the Pacific ocean.
description: Long descriptive sentence with multiple appositive phrases

text: What if the fire were to reach us? We shrugged and laughed. "Guess we'll just die," I'm pretty sure you said.
expected: We shrugged and laughed.
description: Dialogue with attribution after closing quote

text: *So it was*—the epidemic of being in our 20s. Drinking champagne with our shoes off and saying we'd rather be stuck for longer so we'd catch the sunset. But you know, when we sat down, we barely talked—maybe feeling, in a lofty sort of way, that there was no need of words.
expected: Drinking champagne with our shoes off and saying we'd rather be stuck for longer so we'd catch the sunset.
description: Italics at sentence start followed by em dash continuation

text: But you know, when we sat down, we barely talked—maybe feeling, in a lofty sort of way, that there was no need of words. But now I look back and think it was something else, like, we'd set the stage too grand—too grand to invite ordinary conversation. Because I remember, not long after, a cloud briskly overtaking the sun and casting the vista in shade, the novelty of our defiance suddenly seeming to wane, and my next sigh feeling naked and self-conscious.
expected: But now I look back and think it was something else, like, we'd set the stage too grand—too grand to invite ordinary conversation.
description: Em dash with repetitive phrase for emphasis mid-sentence

text: Families in their cars who we fancied wanted to join us (didn't we feel ready to share?), now we imagined rolling their eyes behind us and looking away. We took shyer sips of champagne. But as happens so often—or was it just me?—as quickly as the shade painted over us it seemed, we seemed to paint easily over ourselves, unconsciously recasting our roles and recent history.
expected: We took shyer sips of champagne.
description: Question mark in parenthetical clause mid-sentence does not terminate

text:
But as happens so often—or was it just me?—as quickly as the shade painted over us it seemed, we seemed to paint easily over ourselves, unconsciously recasting our roles and recent history. We were now just bystanders making the best of a situation—sitting hunched and hugging our knees, admiring the view in solemn optimism.  
Ah well, who in their 30s can't relate?
expected: We were now just bystanders making the best of a situation—sitting hunched and hugging our knees, admiring the view in solemn optimism.
description: Paragraph break (double-space newline) between sentences

text: Ah well, who in their 30s can't relate? And something we can all relate to is, ultimately, forgivable—forgivable and forgiven. Sometimes though, we can't relate.
expected: And something we can all relate to is, ultimately, forgivable—forgivable and forgiven.
description: Em dash with word repetition for rhetorical effect

text: Sometimes though, we can't relate. I've wondered a lot these days: Why are we so quick to ostracize someone for an error we simply can't relate to? What fault is it of theirs that we're the ones who don't understand?
expected: I've wondered a lot these days: Why are we so quick to ostracize someone for an error we simply can't relate to?
description: Colon mid-sentence before embedded question

text: I've wondered a lot these days: Why are we so quick to ostracize someone for an error we simply can't relate to? What fault is it of theirs that we're the ones who don't understand? I smile writing this, for the irony of falling back to the "philosophical" language of our teenage years.
expected: What fault is it of theirs that we're the ones who don't understand?
description: Standalone rhetorical question

text: What fault is it of theirs that we're the ones who don't understand? I smile writing this, for the irony of falling back to the "philosophical" language of our teenage years. We spoke it so sincerely, *upon a time*.
expected: I smile writing this, for the irony of falling back to the "philosophical" language of our teenage years.
description: Quoted word mid-sentence does not create boundary

text: I smile writing this, for the irony of falling back to the "philosophical" language of our teenage years. We spoke it so sincerely, *upon a time*. But no, I ask seriously, because... well, I'm writing to you now because I wanted to tell you how, on that day of the wildfire on 101, I *couldn't* tell you how, 2,638 miles away an Uber driver in Manhattan was boarding his flight to come kill me.
expected: We spoke it so sincerely, *upon a time*.
description: Ellipsis mid-sentence does not indicate boundary plus heading markdown in surrounding context

text: I'm sitting here and, I can't seem to get started—starting at the beginning where I should, way back in Kostya's basement. I guess I'm more used to telling a story when I feel like it—when one comes to me to be let out. For me at least, I feel like memories choose their own time to come alive.
expected: I guess I'm more used to telling a story when I feel like it—when one comes to me to be let out.
description: Em dash with when-clause elaboration

text:
For me at least, I feel like memories choose their own time to come alive.  
So let me just start with the one that's knocking now.  
I don't know if you'd remember but, there was a day you agreed to swap shifts with Lindsey and work the afternoon instead of your usual evening.
expected: So let me just start with the one that's knocking now.

text:
So let me just start with the one that's knocking now.

text: So we said we'd hang out when you got home. Those were the days when you'd text me just as you were getting out of work, and I'd order Carmella's and have the food laid out in a circle around your spot on the couch. Oh, you'd definitely remember, because: It was the day you burst through the front door and flung your purse across the room, shouting, "Yay! Home!"—except the purse opened mid-air and everything spilled out all over the living room.
expected: Those were the days when you'd text me just as you were getting out of work, and I'd order Carmella's and have the food laid out in a circle around your spot on the couch.

text: Oh, you'd definitely remember, because: It was the day you burst through the front door and flung your purse across the room, shouting, "Yay! You just laughed and said, "Great! I'm gonna ignore that," and walked straight to the bedroom to change into your comfy clothes.
expected: I remember looking at the debris field and then looking at you with my mouth still hanging open.

text:
To some degree I have to make this up—because I remember feeling feelings and thinking thoughts but not always the exact words I was hearing or saying. On the other hand, there are inconsequential moments I remember in unreasonable detail, like how hard it was to push myself up with one hand—the other hand holding the phone—out of that plush, deep-seated couch of yours—while gesturing to you that I was going to the bedroom—you nodding "of course"—and taking a step when a jolt ran up my leg and I yelled,  
"Ow! F—!"

On the other hand, there are inconsequential moments I remember in unreasonable detail, like how hard it was to push myself up with one hand—the other hand holding the phone—out of that plush, deep-seated couch of yours—while gesturing to you that I was going to the bedroom—you nodding "of course"—and taking a step when a jolt ran up my leg and I yelled,  
"Ow!

text:
"Ow! F—!"  
Whatever it was from your purse—but, that's why I remember it was that day. I remember saying through my teeth, "Sorry, give me one sec," and hurrying to the bedroom, stepping with one edge of my foot, but it wasn't my pain that was on my mind.
expected: Whatever it was from your purse—but, that's why I remember it was that day.

text: But... can you bear with me? It's hard to tell this story at all, because the *fact* of what happened was told in a single sentence on some hospital's notes, but the *way* that it happened wrote all over me. The thing is, around the time of that wildfire and especially the years after, there was this purge in my brain that got underway, burning away memories that, I guess, felt too hard to keep.
expected: It's hard to tell this story at all, because the *fact* of what happened was told in a single sentence on some hospital's notes, but the *way* that it happened wrote all over me.

text: It's hard to tell this story at all, because the *fact* of what happened was told in a single sentence on some hospital's notes, but the *way* that it happened wrote all over me. So bear with me?
